pattern: Only the count of survivors, the value of the current leftmost survivor, and the gap between consecutive survivors ever matter, and each of the two deletion directions updates those three numbers with a simple, direction-dependent rule -- so the whole elimination collapses to a short loop instead of ever materializing the sequence.
hints: Track just three numbers as the round progresses: how many integers remain, what the leftmost remaining integer currently is, and the fixed gap between any two consecutive remaining integers.
Deleting every second number from the left always keeps the current leftmost integer and simply halves (rounding up) how many remain, doubling the gap.

Deleting every second number from the right keeps the leftmost integer unchanged when the remaining count is odd, but shifts the leftmost integer forward by one gap when the remaining count is even -- work out which case applies each round instead of simulating the full list.

edge_inputs.description: statement example three, already a single integer
smallest case with exactly one deletion round
a count that stays odd through the first right-to-left pass
a power of two, so every round splits the remaining count exactly in half
a count one more than a power of two
a moderately large value, still large enough to exercise many elimination rounds

stress.description: random n values across the full magnitude range
n values clustered just above and below powers of two, where the parity logic is most likely to slip
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    p = rng.randint(1, 49)
    base = 1 << p
    offset = rng.randint(-3, 3)
    n = max(1, min(1000000000000000, base + offset))
    return [n]
